MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-08-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT BRIAN HAR, MARK LOBRE, ALLSTATE INSURANCE COMPANY MOTION TO STRIKE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' motion to strike is denied as to the claim for fees. First, Judge Ulmer previously rejected defendant's argument when he partially denied defendants' prior motion to strike and revisiting it now would violate Bennett. Second, the argument fails on the merits. "Section 1021.5 permits a trial court to award attorney's fees to a successful party in any action that '(1) enforced an important public right, (2) conferred a significant public benefit, and (3) is of a type that private enforcement was necessary, and the financial burden justifies subsidizing the successful party's attorneys.'" (Burgess v. Coronado Unified Sch. Dist. (2020) 59 Cal.App.5th 1, 7.) The Court cannot conclude that plaintiff is not entitled to the requested fees as a matter of law merely based on the complaint. Defendants are ultimately raising factual issues and a more fully developed record regarding plaintiff's success, relief obtained, whether a public benefit is conferred, etc. is necessary to resolve the fee issue. The retrospective language of Burgess (e.g. "enforced" public right, "conferred" public benefit) suggests that the private attorney general fee issue is appropriately addressed at the end of the case. Additional caselaw further suggests that this matter should not be resolved on the pleadings. "There is no requirement that the intent to seek attorney fees under section 1021.5 must be pleaded in the underlying action. [Citation.] Such fees are not part of the underlying cause of action, but are incidents to the cause and are properly awarded after entry of a ... judgment[.]" (Washburn v. City of Berkeley (1987) 195 Cal.App.3d 578, 583; see also Snatchko v. Westfield, LLC (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 469, 497 [same].)

The motion to strike is denied as to the claim for punitives. On the one hand, paragraph 41 alleges that defendants issued a check that included "all lien claimants," which could mean that defendants had no intent to harm plaintiff. On the other hand, plaintiff alleges sufficient facts at paragraphs 6-9 regarding defendants' malicious scheme to injure plaintiff. This issue should therefore be resolved by a motion for summary adjudication or the trier of fact.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/SRB)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-08-2022 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS BRIAN HAR, MARK LOBRE, AND ALLSTATE INSURANCE COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' demurrer to plaintiff's second amended complaint for intentional interference with prospective economic advantage and violation of the UCL is overruled.

First, plaintiff alleges a basis for his lien at paragraph 2 of the second amended complaint, e.g., the written fee agreement and/or quantum meruit. There is no requirement that plaintiff attach a copy of his fee agreement at the pleading stage. Such attachment is not even necessary to plead a breach of contract claim, let alone the instant claims. (See Heritage Pacific Financial, LLC v. Monroy (2013) 215 Cal.4th 972, 993 [breach of contract claim may be alleged by attaching a copy of the contract, pleading its terms in haec verba, or pleading its terms by their legal effect]; Korea Supply Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1134, 1153 [elements of intentional interference with prospective advantage cause of action]; Durell v. Sharp Healthcare (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1350, 1361 [pleading UCL claim].) The Court of Appeal did not require such an attachment when it affirmed this court's prior ruling denying a motion for preliminary injunction. That appeal involved an evidentiary motion, not a pleading matter. Second, plaintiff alleges that defendant Allstate is liable on these claims based on its prior conduct in federal litigation. (Second Amended Complaint, par. 3.) Even if this theory of recovery is dubious, defendant never addresses it in its moving papers. "A demurrer does not lie to a portion of a cause of action." (Ellena v. Dep't of Ins. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 198, 217.) Third, defendant's remaining arguments run afoul of the rules against improper reconsideration and piecemeal demurrers. (See Bennett v. Suncloud (1997) 56 Cal.App.4th 91, 96-97 [courts may not reconsider prior rulings on demurrer unless there is compliance with CCP 1008]; CCP 430.41(b) ["A party demurring to a pleading that has been amended after a demurrer to an earlier version of the pleading was sustained shall not demur to any portion of the amended complaint on grounds that could have been raised by demurrer to the earlier version of the complaint[.]")].) Here, Judge Ulmer already ruled that plaintiff could allege a claim for public injunctive relief when he ruled on defendants' demurrer to the first amended complaint and defendants could have, but failed to, previously raise their manageability argument. In any event, the scope of any available injunctive relief ultimately raises a factual issue.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/SRB)

Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Nov-19-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint:

As with any motion, the notice of a demurrer must specify its grounds. (CRC 2.1112(d).) Here, defendants merely recite statutory labels of demurrer types without articulation. Rather than overrule the demurrers on this basis, as it could, the court moves the case forward with the following rulings.

Interference Count. Defendants' main point here seems to be that the first amended complaint (FAC) does not plead how the attorney lien was created. The demurrer is sustained as to this count on that ground with leave to amend.

UCL Count. The FAC adequately pleads a business practice that - if a lien is adequately pled - could be unlawful, unfair and/or fraudulent. Ongoing conduct that threatens plaintiff and other lawyers is also pled. Plaintiff need not file a class action in order to seek a public UCL injunction. (McGill v. Citibank, N.A, (2017) 2 Cal.5th 945, 960-61,) On this count, the demurrer is sustained with leave to amend with a description of how the attorney lien was created, but otherwise overruled.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Plaintiffs Motion For A Preliminary Injunction Motion Under Bus. And Prof. Code Section 17203
Off Calendar Jan-07-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
David Simonton, Esq. of DENTONS US LLP, T:415.267.4000, for defendant;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF BENJAMIN MARTIN's Motion For A Preliminary Injunction Motion Under Business And Professions Code Section 17203, as follows:

Plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction is denied.

First, there is no proof that plaintiff's main target for relief (Allstate Insurance Company) has been served with the complaint or this motion. (See Code Civ. Proc. 527(a) ["No preliminary injunction shall be granted without notice to the opposing party."].)

Second, plaintiff fails to show a likelihood of prevailing on the merits. Competent evidence demonstrates that defendants were not aware of plaintiff's lien when the underlying tort case was settled. (Har Decl., 3.) Further, plaintiff does not demonstrate that Allstate and the Lobre firm have a business practice of thwarting attorney lien rights. Making a settlement check payable jointly to the underlying pro per litigants and "all lien claimants" would appear reasonably calculated to protect lien claimants' rights. (See Post Bros. Constr. Co. v. Yoder (1977) 20 Cal.3d 1, 5-6 ["The joint check rule [in the construction industry] provides a simple yet expeditious method for owner and general contractor to pay their debts to the person with whom they have contracted while eliminating the risk the subcontractor will not pay the person with whom he has contracted."].)

Third, plaintiff's claim is fundamentally about money. If he has suffered economic injury, he has an adequate legal remedy by pursuing his claim for intentional interference with prospective economic advantage. (See Levin v. Gulf Ins. Group (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 1282, 1288.) Lastly, neither the notice of motion nor plaintiff's points and authorities sufficiently sets forth the precise injunctive relief that plaintiff seeks. From what the court can divine, plaintiff vaguely seeks to enjoin the Lobre Firm, Mr. Har, and Allstate from "settling cases without satisfying attorney's liens." (Plaintiff's moving points and authorities, 11:7-8.) The court cannot monitor such an open-ended proscription. (See Continental Baking Co. v. Katz (1968) 68 Cal.2d 512, 534 ["An injunction must not be uncertain or ambiguous and defendant must be able to determine from the order what he may and may not do."].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
